symptom: Popular items get recommended more, which makes them even more popular, and the system keeps training on its own outputs until a shrinking set of winners dominates everything.
framework: Self-reinforcing feedback loops in recommenders. Unlocks exposure/propensity correction, explicit exploration, off-policy debiasing, and long-tail-aware objectives to break the reinforcement cycle.

name: Change blindness
symptom: A user fails to notice a substantial on-screen change — a value updated, an element appeared or vanished — because it happened during a flicker, scroll, page load, or while their attention was elsewhere.
framework: Change-blindness research from visual perception. Unlocks animated transitions that preserve object continuity, salient change highlighting, and avoiding disruptions that mask updates.

name: Gulf of evaluation
symptom: Users can't tell whether their action actually worked or what state the system is now in; or they can't figure out which control maps to the goal they have in mind.
framework: Norman's gulfs of execution and evaluation from The Design of Everyday Things. Unlocks clear feedback, visible system state, natural mappings, and signifiers to bridge intention-to-action and outcome-to-understanding.

name: Novelty effect
symptom: A newly launched feature shows a strong metric lift right after release, then that lift steadily decays over the following weeks as the initial curiosity wears off.
framework: The novelty effect in experimentation. Unlocks longer holdout periods, burn-in windows, cohort/new-vs-returning analysis, and measuring steady-state impact rather than launch-week spikes.

name: Optimizer's curse
symptom: You pick the alternative with the best estimated value, but the realized outcome consistently comes in below its estimate; the 'winner' of your selection process is disappointing on average, again and again.
framework: Described by Smith & Winkler. It's selection bias from maximizing over noisy estimates; the fix is Bayesian shrinkage/hierarchical adjustment of the estimates before you optimize, so the top pick's forecast is de-biased.

name: Flaw of averages
symptom: You plug average inputs into your model and plan around the single expected case, but actual performance is systematically worse (or biased) than that one-number plan promised.
framework: Popularized by Sam Savage; rooted in Jensen's inequality (E[f(X)] ≠ f(E[X])). It points to stochastic programming, scenario analysis, and simulating over full distributions rather than substituting means.

name: Price of anarchy
symptom: Every participant acts in rational self-interest and the system settles into a stable state, yet that state is measurably worse than what a central coordinator could have achieved.
framework: Introduced by Koutsoupias & Papadimitriou. It quantifies the selfish-equilibrium-vs-optimum ratio and motivates mechanism design, tolls, and incentives that realign equilibria with the social optimum.

name: Simplex cycling and degeneracy
symptom: Your LP solver keeps pivoting but the objective value stops improving, sometimes revisiting bases it has already seen, and it can loop indefinitely without making real progress.
framework: A known failure mode of the simplex method at degenerate vertices. The fixes are Bland's anti-cycling rule, lexicographic pivoting, and perturbation techniques that guarantee termination.

name: Ill-conditioning
symptom: Tiny changes in the data or in rounding cause huge swings in the computed solution; the answer feels unstable and untrustworthy, and iterative solvers crawl toward convergence.
framework: Captured by the condition number in numerical linear algebra. Naming it points to preconditioning, variable scaling/normalization, regularization, and numerically stable factorizations.

name: Premature convergence to a local optimum
aliases: getting stuck in a local minimum
symptom: Your search or heuristic settles quickly on a solution and then stops improving no matter how long you run it, yet restarting from different starting points yields different, sometimes much better answers.
framework: The global-vs-local optimization distinction. It unlocks multistart, simulated annealing, tabu search, basin hopping, population methods, and problem convexification/relaxation.

name: No free lunch theorem
symptom: You find an algorithm that dominates every rival on your benchmark, but on other problem classes it does no better than random guessing, and no single method wins everywhere.
framework: Wolpert & Macready's result: averaged over all problems, all optimizers perform equally. It reframes the goal as matching algorithm to problem structure and exploiting known priors instead of seeking a universal solver.

name: Combinatorial explosion (NP-hardness)
symptom: A problem that's trivial on small instances becomes impossible as size grows; the number of feasible combinations explodes exponentially and brute-force enumeration is hopeless.
framework: Computational complexity theory / NP-hardness. Recognizing it steers you to branch-and-bound, cutting planes, approximation algorithms with guarantees, metaheuristics, or reformulation to exploit special structure.

name: Newsvendor problem
symptom: You must commit to a quantity before uncertain demand is revealed: order too much and you eat waste/markdown, order too little and you lose sales — and simply ordering the mean demand turns out not to be optimal.
framework: The classic single-period inventory model. The name gives the critical-fractile solution: order up to F⁻¹(Cu/(Cu+Co)), balancing underage against overage cost.

name: Variability-induced congestion (Kingman's formula)
symptom: As you push a resource's utilization toward 100% to seem 'efficient,' waiting times and queue lengths don't rise gently — they blow up nonlinearly, and any variability in arrivals or service makes it dramatically worse.
framework: Queueing theory and Kingman's VUT (variability–utilization–time) equation: wait scales with ρ/(1−ρ) times a variability factor. It prescribes buffer capacity, variability reduction, and not running systems near saturation.

name: Big-M formulation weakness
symptom: Your mixed-integer model uses large constant coefficients to switch constraints on and off; it solves slowly, gives loose relaxation bounds, and can even return solutions that violate the intended logic because of solver tolerances.
framework: A well-known modeling trap in integer programming. The fix is to tighten the constants to their smallest valid values, or replace them with indicator constraints or convex-hull/disaggregated (extended) formulations.

name: Zigzagging of steepest descent
symptom: Your gradient-based optimizer bounces back and forth across the walls of a long, narrow, curved valley, taking tiny steps down its length and crawling agonizingly slowly toward the minimum.
framework: The classic ill-conditioned descent behavior illustrated by the Rosenbrock function. It points to momentum, conjugate-gradient, Newton/quasi-Newton (curvature) methods, and preconditioning/rescaling.

name: Exploration–exploitation dilemma
symptom: You keep choosing the option that looks best so far, but you're never sure whether a less-tried option is actually better; test too long and you burn resources on inferior choices, commit too early and you may lock into a suboptimal one.
framework: The multi-armed bandit framework. Naming it unlocks regret-minimizing policies: upper-confidence-bound (UCB), Thompson sampling, and epsilon-greedy strategies that balance learning against earning.

name: Integrality gap
symptom: You drop the integer constraints to get a quick bound, but the fractional solution's value sits far from any achievable whole-number solution, so the bound is loose and naive rounding produces poor results.
framework: The gap between an integer program and its LP relaxation. It motivates stronger/tighter formulations, valid inequalities and cutting planes, branch-and-cut, and it bounds achievable approximation ratios.
